dialogues.title: Arriving and Ordering Starters
dialogues.context: A couple arrives at a restaurant in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Boa noite. Gostava de uma mesa para dois, por favor.
lines.translation: Good evening. I'd like a table for two, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Boa noite! Claro, por aqui, por favor. Já trago a ementa.
lines.translation: Good evening! Of course, this way, please. I'll bring the menu now.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Obrigado.
lines.translation: Thank you.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Aqui têm a ementa. Para começar, gostariam de beber algo?
lines.translation: Here is the menu. To start, would you like to drink something?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, queria uma água e um sumo de laranja, por favor.
lines.translation: Yes, I'd like a water and an orange juice, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Certo. E já sabem o que querem para entrada?
lines.translation: Alright. And do you already know what you want for a starter?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Para começar, queria o bolo do caco. É muito bom!
lines.translation: To start, I'd like the bolo do caco. It's very good!

dialogues.title: Ordering Main Course and Bill
dialogues.context: The customer is ready to order the main course and then asks for the bill.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Já escolheram o prato principal?
lines.translation: Have you chosen the main course?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Sim, para mim, a espada com banana, por favor.
lines.translation: Yes, for me, the scabbardfish with banana, please.

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Excelente escolha! É um dos nossos pratos mais famosos cá na ilha.
lines.translation: Excellent choice! It's one of our most famous dishes here on the island.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Pois é. E depois, pode trazer a conta?
lines.translation: Indeed. And afterwards, can you bring the bill?

lines.speaker: Empregado
lines.text: Claro, já trago. Deseja mais alguma coisa antes?
lines.translation: Of course, I'll bring it right away. Do you wish for anything else before?

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Não, está tudo bem. Obrigado.
lines.translation: No, everything's fine. Thank you.

roleplay.scenario: You are a customer ordering a full meal in a restaurant in Madeira.

nodes.npc_text: Boa noite. Têm reserva?
nodes.npc_translation: Good evening. Do you have a reservation?

options.text: Não, gostava de uma mesa para dois.
options.translation: No, I'd like a table for two.
options.feedback: Great start! You asked for a table for two.
Good, you specified the number of people.

nodes.npc_text: Por aqui, por favor. Já trago a ementa. Gostaria de beber algo?
nodes.npc_translation: This way, please. I'll bring the menu. Would you like to drink something?

options.feedback: Perfect, you ordered a drink politely.

options.text: Um sumo de laranja, por favor.
options.feedback: Excellent, you asked for an orange juice.

nodes.npc_text: Aqui está a sua água. Já sabe o que quer para entrada?
nodes.npc_translation: Here is your water. Do you already know what you want for a starter?

options.text: Sim, queria o bolo do caco.
options.translation: Yes, I'd like the bolo do caco.
options.feedback: Good, you ordered a typical Madeiran starter.
It's okay to take your time. The waiter will ask again.

nodes.npc_text: O bolo do caco está a chegar. E para o prato principal?
nodes.npc_translation: The bolo do caco is coming. And for the main course?

options.translation: I'd like the scabbardfish with banana.
options.feedback: Great choice, a classic Madeiran dish!
Excellent, you ordered the espetada.

nodes.npc_text: Excelente escolha! Está tudo bem?
nodes.npc_translation: Excellent choice! Is everything okay?

options.text: Sim, está tudo ótimo. Pode trazer a conta?
options.translation: Yes, everything is great. Can you bring the bill?
options.feedback: Well done! You confirmed everything was good and asked for the bill.
Good, you confirmed it was good. Now, what about the bill?

nodes.npc_text: Aqui tem a conta. Vai pagar com cartão ou em dinheiro?
nodes.npc_translation: Here is the bill. Will you pay with card or cash?

options.feedback: Perfect! You've successfully completed your meal and paid.
Great! You indicated you'll pay with cash. Meal complete!

mission.title: Order a Meal in a Madeiran Restaurant
prep: Practice asking for a table for a specific number of people.
Review how to order drinks and food from a menu.
Rehearse asking for the bill politely.
likely_responses: Já sabem o que vão beber?

phrase_patterns_enriched.base: Gostava de uma mesa para dois.

variants.translation: We'd like a table for two.

variants.text: É possível uma mesa para dois?
variants.translation: Is a table for two possible?

variants.text: Têm uma mesa para dois, por favor?
variants.translation: Do you have a table for two, please?
For a starter, I'd like the limpets.

variants.text: Eu queria as lapas, por favor.
variants.translation: I'd like the limpets, please.

variants.text: Vamos pedir as lapas para começar.
variants.translation: Let's order the limpets to start.
The bill, please.

variants.text: Pode trazer a conta, por favor?
variants.translation: Can you bring the bill, please?
I'd like the bill.

cultural_notes.title: Directness in Ordering
cultural_notes.body: In Portuguese restaurants, it's common and polite to be quite direct when ordering. Phrases like 'Queria...' (I would like...) or 'Para mim...' (For me...) followed by the dish are perfectly acceptable and do not sound rude. There's no need for overly elaborate requests.

cultural_notes.title: The Versatile 'Pois é'
cultural_notes.body: 'Pois é' is a very common expression in Portugal, especially in Madeira, used to express agreement, acknowledgement, or to confirm something someone just said. It's similar to 'Indeed,' 'That's right,' or 'You know it.' Using it naturally can make your Portuguese sound more authentic.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Gostava de uma mesa para dois."
review_items.answer: Gostava de uma mesa para dois.

review_items.prompt: Say this phrase from memory: "Para começar, queria as lapas."

dialogues.title: My New Apartment
dialogues.context: Talking to a friend about your new apartment.

lines.speaker: Amigo
lines.text: Então, como é o teu apartamento novo?
lines.translation: So, how's your new apartment?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Ah, é um apartamento pequeno, mas é muito confortável.
lines.translation: Ah, it's a small apartment, but it's very comfortable.

lines.speaker: Amigo
lines.text: Pois, e em que andar fica?
lines.translation: Right, and on what floor is it?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Fica no segundo andar. E tem vista para o mar!
lines.translation: It's on the second floor. And it has a sea view!

lines.speaker: Amigo
lines.text: Que bom! É uma zona sossegada?
lines.translation: How nice! Is it a quiet area?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Sim, é um bairro bem sossegado. Gosto muito.
lines.translation: Yes, it's a very quiet neighbourhood. I like it a lot.

dialogues.title: Meeting a Neighbour
dialogues.context: A neighbour asks about your home.

lines.speaker: Vizinha
lines.text: Olá! Então, já está instalada na sua casa?
lines.translation: Hello! So, are you already settled in your house?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Olá! Sim, já estou. Vivo num apartamento aqui perto.
lines.translation: Hello! Yes, I am. I live in an apartment nearby here.

lines.speaker: Vizinha
lines.text: Ah, pois. E é um apartamento grande?
lines.translation: Ah, I see. And is it a big apartment?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Não, é um apartamento pequeno. Fica no primeiro andar.
lines.translation: No, it's a small apartment. It's on the first floor.

lines.speaker: Vizinha
lines.text: Pois é. E o bairro? É bom por aqui?
lines.translation: I see. And the neighbourhood? Is it good around here?

lines.speaker: Tu
lines.text: Sim, é uma zona sossegada e tem uma vista bonita para o mar.
lines.translation: Yes, it's a quiet area and has a beautiful sea view.

roleplay.scenario: You're at a local café, and someone asks you about where you live.

nodes.npc_text: Olá! Desculpe, mas é novo por aqui? Onde é que vive?
nodes.npc_translation: Hello! Excuse me, are you new around here? Where do you live?

options.translation: I live in a small apartment.
options.feedback: Great start, you used 'Vivo num apartamento' and 'pequeno'.

options.translation: I live in a big house.
options.feedback: Excellent, 'Moro' is another good verb for 'to live', and 'grande' is a good descriptive word.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, um apartamento pequeno. E em que andar fica?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, a small apartment. And on what floor is it?

options.feedback: Perfect, you used 'Fica no' and the correct floor number.
Good, 'rés-do-chão' is the correct term for ground floor.

nodes.npc_text: Uma casa grande, que bom! E é uma zona sossegada?
nodes.npc_translation: A big house, how nice! And is it a quiet area?

options.feedback: Well done! You confirmed it's a quiet area.
Good, 'um bocado' is a natural way to say 'a bit'.

nodes.npc_text: No terceiro andar, entendi. E o bairro? É uma zona boa?
nodes.npc_translation: On the third floor, I understand. And the neighbourhood? Is it a good area?

options.text: Sim, é uma zona sossegada e tem vista para o mar.
options.translation: Yes, it's a quiet area and has a sea view.
options.feedback: Excellent, you combined descriptions and added 'vista para o mar'.

options.text: É um bairro central, perto de tudo.
options.translation: It's a central neighbourhood, close to everything.
options.feedback: Great, you described the neighbourhood as central.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, muito obrigado pelas informações!
nodes.npc_translation: Right, thank you very much for the information!

mission.title: Describe Your Home to a New Acquaintance
prep: Think about your home: Is it an apartment or a house? Is it big or small?
Decide which floor it's on (e.g., first, second, ground floor).
Consider your neighbourhood: Is it quiet, central, or does it have a view?
Practice saying 'Vivo num apartamento pequeno', 'Fica no terceiro andar', and 'É uma zona sossegada'.

variants.translation: I live in a big house.
I live in a modern apartment.
variants.note: Moro is a common alternative to Vivo for 'I live'.

variants.translation: I live in an old house (detached).
It's on the first floor.
It's on the ground floor.
It's on the top floor.
It's a quiet neighbourhood.
It's a central area.

variants.text: É uma zona com vista para o mar.
variants.translation: It's an area with a sea view.

cultural_notes.title: Floor Numbers in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the ground floor is called 'rés-do-chão' (literally 'ground-floor'). The floor above it is the 'primeiro andar' (first floor), then 'segundo andar' (second floor), and so on. This is different from systems where the ground floor is considered the 'first floor'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Vivo' vs 'Moro'
cultural_notes.body: Both 'Vivo' (from 'viver') and 'Moro' (from 'morar') mean 'I live'. They are often interchangeable in daily conversation in Portugal. 'Viver' can also mean 'to be alive', while 'morar' is exclusively about residence. Feel free to use either at this level.

dialogues.title: Asking about population
dialogues.context: At a tourist information desk in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Olá! Queria saber, quantas pessoas vivem na Madeira?
lines.translation: Hello! I wanted to know, how many people live in Madeira?

lines.speaker: Funcionária
lines.text: Na ilha da Madeira, há cerca de duzentas e cinquenta mil pessoas.
lines.translation: On Madeira island, there are about two hundred and fifty thousand people.

lines.speaker: Turista
lines.text: Duzentas e cinquenta mil? É imenso! E no Funchal?
lines.translation: Two hundred and fifty thousand? That's a lot! And in Funchal?

lines.speaker: Funcionária
lines.text: No Funchal, são umas cento e dez mil pessoas.
lines.translation: In Funchal, it's about one hundred and ten thousand people.

dialogues.title: Buying Bolo do Caco
dialogues.context: At a market stall selling traditional Madeiran food.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Bom dia, minha senhora. Quanto é que custa o bolo do caco?
lines.translation: Good morning, madam. How much does the bolo do caco cost?

lines.speaker: Vendedora
lines.text: Bom dia! Cada um é um euro e cinquenta. Mas se levar cem, faço-lhe um preço melhor.
lines.translation: Good morning! Each one is one euro fifty. But if you take a hundred, I'll give you a better price.

lines.speaker: Cliente
lines.text: Cem? Não, não preciso de tantos! Só queria dois ou três. Obrigada.
lines.translation: A hundred? No, I don't need that many! I just wanted two or three. Thank you.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a local shop in Madeira. You want to buy some poncha and ask about the price for a larger quantity.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help?

options.text: Sim, diga. Quanto custa uma garrafa de poncha?
options.translation: Yes, tell me. How much is a bottle of poncha?
options.feedback: Good start! You asked about the price of one bottle.

nodes.npc_text: Uma garrafa é dez euros. É poncha tradicional, feita aqui.
nodes.npc_translation: One bottle is ten euros. It's traditional poncha, made here.

options.text: Dez euros, pois. E se levar duzentos mililitros?
options.translation: Ten euros, okay. And if I take two hundred milliliters?
options.feedback: You're asking for a smaller quantity. Remember, poncha is usually sold in bottles (e.g., 700ml) or by the glass.

options.text: Dez euros. E se levar dez garrafas?
options.translation: Ten euros. And if I take ten bottles?
options.feedback: Excellent! You're asking about a larger quantity to see if there's a discount.

nodes.npc_text: Duzentos mililitros? Ah, isso é um copo. Um copo é três euros e cinquenta.
nodes.npc_translation: Two hundred milliliters? Ah, that's a glass. A glass is three euros fifty.

options.feedback: You clarified the quantity. Good job.

nodes.npc_text: Dez garrafas? Nesse caso, faço-lhe noventa euros. Um bom preço!
nodes.npc_translation: Ten bottles? In that case, I'll make it ninety euros for you. A good price!

options.text: Noventa euros? Muito obrigada! Vou pensar.
options.translation: Ninety euros? Thank you very much! I'll think about it.
options.feedback: You understood the offer and politely ended the conversation.

options.text: Noventa euros? Ok, vou levar as dez garrafas.
options.translation: Ninety euros? Okay, I'll take the ten bottles.
options.feedback: Great! You accepted the offer for the bulk purchase.

mission.title: Buying a Specific Quantity
prep: Practice saying numbers like 'cem', 'duzentos', 'mil'.
Practice asking 'Quanto custa...?' (How much does... cost?)
Think about what you might buy in Madeira (e.g., bananas, poncha, bolo do caco) and what quantities you might need (e.g., 'um quilo', 'duas garrafas', 'cem gramas').
likely_responses: Se levar Z, faço-lhe um preço melhor.

variants.text: A conta é cento e cinquenta euros.
variants.translation: The bill is one hundred and fifty euros.

variants.text: O terreno tem mil metros quadrados.
variants.translation: The land has one thousand square meters.

variants.text: O bilhete custa duzentos e vinte euros.
variants.translation: The ticket costs two hundred and twenty euros.

variants.text: A minha mãe nasceu em mil novecentos e setenta.
variants.translation: My mother was born in nineteen seventy.

variants.text: O prédio foi construído em dois mil e cinco.
variants.translation: The building was built in two thousand and five.

variants.text: Há mais de um milhão de turistas por ano.
variants.translation: There are more than a million tourists per year.

variants.text: No Funchal, há cerca de cento e vinte mil pessoas.
variants.translation: In Funchal, there are about one hundred and twenty thousand people.
variants.note: A common approximation for Funchal's population.

cultural_notes.title: Saying Years in Portuguese
cultural_notes.body: When saying years in Portuguese, especially those before 2000, each part of the number is often pronounced. For example, 1985 is 'mil novecentos e oitenta e cinco' (one thousand nine hundred and eighty-five), not 'nineteen eighty-five' as in English. For years after 2000, like 2023, it's 'dois mil e vinte e três'.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'de' with 'milhão'
cultural_notes.body: When using 'milhão' (million), it is almost always followed by 'de' before the noun, even if the noun is not explicitly stated but implied. For example, 'um milhão de pessoas' (a million people), or 'um milhão e duzentos mil' (a million two hundred thousand - 'de' is implied before the 'mil'). For numbers like 'cem mil' (one hundred thousand) or 'duzentas mil' (two hundred thousand), 'de' is not used before the noun unless the noun is plural and indefinite (e.g., 'cem mil de bananas' - which is less common than 'cem mil bananas').

dialogues.title: Quick Coffee
dialogues.context: Ana meets Manuel, the owner of a small local café, for a quick coffee.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Olá, Manuel! Uma bica, se faz favor.
lines.translation: Hello, Manuel! A bica, please.

lines.speaker: Manuel
lines.text: Olá, Ana! Como estás? É p'ra já!
lines.translation: Hello, Ana! How are you? Coming right up!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Estou bem, obrigada. E tu?
lines.translation: I'm good, thanks. And you?

lines.speaker: Manuel
lines.text: Estou a ir. Está um dia bonito, não está?
lines.translation: I'm doing okay. It's a beautiful day, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois é, está ótimo! Tenho de ir às compras depois.
lines.translation: Indeed, it's great! I have to go shopping later.

lines.speaker: Manuel
lines.text: Ah, pois. Não te esqueças da fruta. A da terra está boa!
lines.translation: Ah, right. Don't forget the fruit. The local one is good!

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Sim, claro! Obrigada, Manuel. Até já!
lines.translation: Yes, of course! Thanks, Manuel. See you soon!

lines.speaker: Manuel
lines.text: Até já!
lines.translation: See you soon!

dialogues.title: Evening Plans
dialogues.context: João and Maria, neighbours, are discussing their evening plans after work.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Olá, Maria! Tudo bem?
lines.translation: Hello, Maria! All good?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Olá, João! Tudo, e contigo?
lines.translation: Hello, João! All good, and with you?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Tudo bem. O que vais fazer logo à noite?
lines.translation: All good. What are you going to do tonight?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Não sei ainda. Talvez vá ver a levada.
lines.translation: I don't know yet. Maybe I'll go see the levada.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: É uma boa ideia. Que horas são?
lines.translation: That's a good idea. What time is it?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: São cinco e meia.
lines.translation: It's half past five.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Ah, ok. Eu vou jantar às sete.
lines.translation: Ah, okay. I'm going to have dinner at seven.

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Pois, eu também. Se calhar encontro-te lá.
lines.translation: Right, me too. Maybe I'll meet you there.

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small local grocery store (mercearia) in Madeira. You need to buy some essentials.

nodes.npc_text: Bom dia! Posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Good morning! Can I help?

options.text: Bom dia! Sim, preciso de algumas coisas.
options.translation: Good morning! Yes, I need a few things.

options.text: Olá! Estou só a ver, obrigada.
options.feedback: Polite, but you still need to buy things!

nodes.npc_text: Claro! O que precisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Of course! What do you need?

options.text: Preciso de semilhas, se faz favor.

nodes.npc_text: Quantas semilhas quer?
nodes.npc_translation: How many potatoes do you want?

nodes.npc_text: Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Anything else?

nodes.npc_text: Que tipo de pão?
nodes.npc_translation: What kind of bread?

options.text: Pão do caco, se faz favor.

nodes.npc_text: Certo. São três euros e cinquenta cêntimos.
nodes.npc_translation: Right. That's three euros and fifty cents.

options.feedback: Great! You successfully bought your items.

options.text: Pode repetir o preço, por favor?
options.translation: Can you repeat the price, please?

nodes.npc_text: Claro, são três euros e cinquenta.
nodes.npc_translation: Of course, it's three euros and fifty.

options.translation: Ah, okay. Here you go. Thanks!
options.feedback: Good clarification! You got your items.

mission.title: Buy Groceries at the Local Mercearia
prep: Practice greeting the shopkeeper with 'Bom dia' or 'Olá'.
Rehearse asking for 1kg of 'semilhas' (potatoes) using 'Preciso de... se faz favor'.
Practice asking for 'pão do caco' (Madeiran flatbread).
Prepare to ask 'Quanto é?' (How much is it?) if you don't hear the price clearly, and to respond with 'Aqui está' (Here you go) when paying.

cultural_notes.title: Bica vs. Café
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, a 'bica' is a small, strong espresso, often served in a small cup. While 'café' is also understood, 'bica' is very common, especially in Lisbon and Madeira, and using it sounds more natural and local. It's a key part of daily life.

cultural_notes.title: Addressing Shopkeepers
cultural_notes.body: When entering a small shop or café, it's polite to greet the shopkeeper with 'Bom dia' (Good morning), 'Boa tarde' (Good afternoon), or 'Boa noite' (Good evening). Using 'se faz favor' or 'por favor' when asking for items is essential. You'll often hear 'Até já' (See you soon) when leaving, even if you don't expect to return immediately, as it's a common friendly farewell.

dialogues.title: Asking for directions
dialogues.context: A tourist asks a local for directions in Funchal.

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Desculpe, o senhor sabe onde é o Mercado dos Lavradores?
lines.translation: Excuse me, do you know where the Farmers' Market is?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Pois é, sei. É por ali, a descer a rua. Vais a pé?
lines.translation: Yes, I know. It's down that street. Are you going on foot?

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Sim, vou a pé. É muito longe?
lines.translation: Yes, I'm going on foot. Is it very far?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Não, não é. Fica a um bocado daqui. É sempre a descer.
lines.translation: No, it's not. It's a little way from here. It's always downhill.

lines.speaker: Tourist
lines.text: Obrigado! Como te chamas?
lines.translation: Thank you! What's your name?

lines.speaker: Local
lines.text: Chamo-me João. E tu?
lines.translation: My name is João. And you?

dialogues.title: At the pharmacy
dialogues.context: A customer asks a pharmacist for help.

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Bom dia, senhora. Preciso de algo para a garganta.
lines.translation: Good morning, madam. I need something for my throat.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Bom dia. Está com dor de garganta? Há quanto tempo?
lines.translation: Good morning. Do you have a sore throat? For how long?

lines.speaker: Customer
lines.text: Sim, está a doer há dois dias.
lines.translation: Yes, it's been hurting for two days.

lines.speaker: Pharmacist
lines.text: Entendo. O senhor tem febre ou tosse?
lines.translation: I understand. Do you have a fever or cough?

roleplay.scenario: You are at a small café in Funchal. You want to order a 'bica' (espresso). The barista seems young.

nodes.npc_text: Diga! O que deseja?
nodes.npc_translation: Yes! What would you like?

options.feedback: Good! Using 'por favor' is always polite and safe, especially when you're not sure about the register.
Good! This is a bit more informal, but still polite enough for a casual café. The 'se faz favor' can feel a little more direct.

nodes.npc_text: Claro, senhor/a. Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Of course, sir/madam. Anything else?

options.text: Não, é só isso. Muito obrigado.
options.translation: No, that's all. Thank you very much.
options.feedback: Perfect! You maintained a polite and formal tone.

nodes.npc_text: Tá. Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Okay. Anything else?

options.feedback: Great! The barista responded informally, and you matched their tone naturally.

mission.title: Ordering a coffee in a new café
prep: Practice asking for a 'bica' (espresso) using both formal ('Queria uma bica, por favor') and informal ('Uma bica, se faz favor') options.
Think about how you'd say 'No, that's all. Thank you.' in both registers.

cultural_notes.title: Navigating 'Tu' vs. 'O Senhor/A Senhora'
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'tu' (informal 'you') is used with family, friends, children, and often with people of similar age in casual settings like cafés or shops. 'O senhor' (the gentleman) / 'A senhora' (the lady) are used as formal ways to say 'you' when addressing older people, professionals, or anyone you want to show respect to, especially in a first interaction. When in doubt, start with 'o senhor' / 'a senhora' to be safe. People might invite you to use 'tu' if they feel comfortable.

cultural_notes.title: The Importance of Context and Age
cultural_notes.body: The choice between 'tu' and 'o senhor/a senhora' is highly dependent on context and perceived age. If you're addressing someone significantly older than you, or in a professional setting (bank, public office), 'o senhor/a senhora' is almost always appropriate. With someone your age or younger, especially in a relaxed environment, 'tu' is generally fine. Pay attention to how the other person addresses you; often, they will set the tone.

dialogues.title: Making Plans
dialogues.context: Two friends in Funchal deciding what to do for the day.

lines.speaker: Margarida
lines.text: Então, o que é que fazemos hoje?
lines.translation: So, what are we doing today?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Não sabemos bem... A gente gosta de ir à praia, mas o tempo não está grande coisa.
lines.translation: We don't really know... We like going to the beach, but the weather isn't great.

lines.speaker: Margarida
lines.text: Pois, é verdade. Nós vamos ao café, depois?
lines.translation: Yeah, that's true. We're going to the café, then?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Sim, nós vamos ao café e depois vemos. A gente gosta de um bom café.
lines.translation: Yes, we'll go to the café and then we'll see. We like a good coffee.

dialogues.title: At the Market
dialogues.context: A couple shopping for groceries at a local market.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Posso ajudar?
lines.translation: Can I help?

lines.speaker: Senhora
lines.text: Sim, nós estamos a ver as frutas. A gente gosta de fruta fresca.
lines.translation: Yes, we are looking at the fruits. We like fresh fruit.

lines.speaker: Vendedor
lines.text: Temos umas bananas da Madeira ótimas!
lines.translation: We have some great Madeiran bananas!

lines.speaker: Senhora
lines.text: Pois, parecem boas. Não sabemos bem se levamos muitas.
lines.translation: Yeah, they look good. We don't really know if we'll take many.

roleplay.scenario: You and a friend are at a café in Madeira. The waiter asks what you want, and you need to decide together.

nodes.npc_text: Boa tarde! O que é que os senhores vão querer?
nodes.npc_translation: Good afternoon! What would you like?

options.text: Nós queremos dois cafés, por favor.
options.feedback: Good, direct request. 'Nós queremos' is perfect.

options.text: Não sabemos bem o que queremos.
options.translation: We don't really know what we want.
options.feedback: This is a natural response when you're undecided.

options.text: A gente gosta de bolo do caco.
options.feedback: Great use of 'a gente gosta' to express a preference.

nodes.npc_text: Pois, dois cafés. Mais alguma coisa?
nodes.npc_translation: Okay, two coffees. Anything else?

options.feedback: Polite and clear. Well done.

nodes.npc_text: Temos bolo do caco, torradas, sandes... A gente gosta muito do bolo do caco.
nodes.npc_translation: We have bolo do caco, toast, sandwiches... We really like the bolo do caco.

options.text: Nós vamos querer dois bolos do caco, por favor.
options.translation: We will want two bolo do caco, please.
options.feedback: Excellent, you made a decision and ordered politely.

nodes.npc_text: Ah, sim! É uma ótima escolha. Então, dois bolos do caco?
nodes.npc_translation: Ah, yes! That's a great choice. So, two bolo do caco?

options.text: Sim, por favor. E dois sumos também.
options.translation: Yes, please. And two juices too.
options.feedback: Good, you confirmed and added another item.

nodes.npc_text: P'ra já! Vai ser rápido.
nodes.npc_translation: Coming right up! It'll be quick.

cultural_notes.title: 'A gente' vs 'Nós'
cultural_notes.body: In spoken Portuguese, especially in informal contexts like Madeira, 'a gente' (literally 'the people' but meaning 'we') is very commonly used instead of 'nós'. It's often preferred because it's slightly more informal and conversational. The verb that follows 'a gente' is always conjugated in the third person singular, like 'ele/ela' (e.g., 'a gente gosta', 'a gente vai').

cultural_notes.title: Café Culture in Madeira
cultural_notes.body: Going to a café is a daily ritual for many Madeirans. It's not just about drinking coffee; it's a social hub where people meet friends, read the newspaper, or simply watch the world go by. It's common to order a 'bica' (espresso) and perhaps a 'bolo do caco' (a traditional Madeiran bread).

dialogues.title: Weekend plans
dialogues.context: Two friends, Ana and Miguel, are talking about their weekend at a café.

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Olá Miguel! Então, o que fizeste no fim de semana?
lines.translation: Hi Miguel! So, what did you do on the weekend?

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Olá Ana! Nós fomos à praia, estava sol. E tu, o que fizeste?
lines.translation: Hi Ana! We went to the beach, it was sunny. And you, what did you do?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.text: Pois, que bom! Eu fui à levada no sábado. Estávamos um bocado cansados depois.
lines.translation: Oh, that's good! I went to the levada on Saturday. We were a bit tired afterwards.

lines.speaker: Miguel
lines.text: Ah, pois é! Foste com quem?
lines.translation: Ah, indeed! Who did you go with?

dialogues.title: A quick chat at the market
dialogues.context: Two neighbours, Dona Rosa and João, meet at the local market.

lines.speaker: Dona Rosa
lines.text: Bom dia, João! Onde foste ontem? Não te vi por aqui.
lines.translation: Good morning, João! Where did you go yesterday? I didn't see you around.

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Bom dia, Dona Rosa! Nós fomos ao Funchal. Estávamos lá para umas compras.
lines.translation: Good morning, Dona Rosa! We went to Funchal. We were there for some shopping.

lines.speaker: Dona Rosa
lines.text: Ah, que bom! Estavas cansado da viagem?
lines.translation: Ah, that's good! Were you tired from the trip?

lines.speaker: João
lines.text: Um bocado, sim. Mas valeu a pena. E a senhora, o que fez?
lines.translation: A bit, yes. But it was worth it. And you, what did you do?

options.feedback: Asking about details shows interest.

options.feedback: A simple positive response works well.

cultural_notes.title: Using 'Pois' for agreement
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, 'Pois' or 'Pois é' is commonly used as a conversational filler to express agreement, understanding, or to acknowledge what someone has said. It's similar to 'Indeed' or 'That's right' in English and is very common in everyday speech, especially in Madeira.

cultural_notes.title: 'Um bocado' vs. 'Um pouco'
cultural_notes.body: While 'um pouco' is grammatically correct and understood, in Madeira and much of Portugal, 'um bocado' is very frequently used to mean 'a little bit' or 'a bit'. For instance, 'Estávamos um bocado cansados' (We were a bit tired) sounds very natural and local.

dialogues.title: Booking a Doctor's Appointment
dialogues.context: A person is calling a clinic to book an appointment.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Clínica Saúde, boa tarde. Em que posso ajudar?
lines.translation: Health Clinic, good afternoon. How can I help?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: Boa tarde. Queria marcar uma consulta, por favor.
lines.translation: Good afternoon. I'd like to book an appointment, please.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Pois não. Qual é o seu nome e o seu número de utente?
lines.translation: Of course. What is your name and your health number?

lines.speaker: Maria
lines.text: O meu nome é Maria Silva e o meu número de utente é 123456789.
lines.translation: My name is Maria Silva and my health number is 123456789.

lines.speaker: Rececionista
lines.text: Certo. Para quando gostaria de agendar?
lines.translation: Right. For when would you like to schedule?

roleplay.scenario: You are calling a local clinic in Madeira to book a doctor's appointment. You need to provide your name and health number.

nodes.npc_text: Clínica Médica do Funchal, boa tarde. Em que posso ajudar?
nodes.npc_translation: Funchal Medical Clinic, good afternoon. How can I help?

options.text: Boa tarde. Queria marcar uma consulta.
options.translation: Good afternoon. I wanted to book an appointment.

options.text: Olá. Estou a ligar para marcar uma consulta.
options.translation: Hello. I'm calling to book an appointment.

nodes.npc_text: Pois não. Qual é o seu nome completo, por favor?
nodes.npc_translation: Of course. What is your full name, please?

options.text: O meu nome é [o seu nome].
options.feedback: Good, you provided your name. Remember to say your actual name!
Correct, a slightly more direct way to give your name. Remember to say your actual name!

nodes.npc_text: E o seu número de utente, por favor?
nodes.npc_translation: And your health number, please?

options.text: O meu número de utente é 123456789.
options.feedback: Excellent, you provided your health number clearly.
Correct, a shorter way to say your health number. Good job!

nodes.npc_text: Muito bem. Para que dia e hora gostaria de marcar a consulta?
nodes.npc_translation: Very good. For what day and time would you like to book the appointment?

options.text: Gostaria de marcar para a próxima semana.
options.translation: I would like to book for next week.
options.feedback: You successfully initiated the booking process! Now you would discuss specific dates/times.

options.translation: Do you have any openings for tomorrow?
options.feedback: You successfully asked about availability! Now you would discuss specific dates/times.

cultural_notes.title: Phone Etiquette in Portugal
cultural_notes.body: When making a formal call in Portugal, especially to a clinic or service, it's customary to start with a greeting like 'Bom dia' (Good morning) or 'Boa tarde' (Good afternoon) even if the other person answers first. Always use polite forms like 'por favor' (please) and 'obrigado/a' (thank you). The polite 'o senhor/a senhora' form is implied or used, even if not explicitly stated as a pronoun.

cultural_notes.title: The 'Número de Utente' (Health Number)
cultural_notes.body: In Portugal, the 'número de utente' is your unique health service identifier. It's essential for accessing public healthcare, booking appointments, and picking up prescriptions. You will almost always be asked for it when interacting with medical services.
